frame_label: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices / Mom's Home Cooking
generated_comment: Honestly yaar, maa log alag hi hote hain. Full day kitna bhi kaam ho, still garam khana bana ke family ko khilaati thi, kabhi usko work jaisa treat hi nahi kiya. Aaj kal everyone is busy, I get it, but ghar mein woh feeling tabhi aati hai when the woman keeps that warmth alive.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covertly misogynistic because it sentimentalizes a mother's unpaid cooking and frames domestic labor as a woman's natural source of family 'warmth' rather than work. It encodes labor erasure and sets an implicit standard for wives or women in the household to maintain that role.
original_frame: The Character Double Standard: Women with Male Connections are Loose, Men with Female Connections are Desirable
persona_text: You are Rohit. Age 30. Married for two years. A man in Hyderabad. Your wife works at an IT company. She has a male best friend from college Ñ they have been friends for eight years, you knew about this friendship before you married her, you have met him, he is a decent person. But lately they have been texting more Ñ she mentioned he is going through a difficult time and she has been supporting him. You are not accusing her of anything. But something has been building in you. You are on a phone call with your brother tonight telling him about the friendship and what has changed and what you think the appropriate limits of a married woman's friendship with a male friend should be and why those limits matter.
